content:
# Matthew Chapter 6 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Matthew Chapter 6 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 6:1 — "Beware of practicing your righteousness" vs. "Take heed the righteousness of you not to do" vs. "Be careful not to perform your acts of righteousness"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Take heed the righteousness of you not to do before the men" | Extremely literal with awkward word order |
| LOGOS | "Beware of practicing your righteousness before others in order to be seen by them" | Formal, natural English |
| PNEUMA | "Be careful not to perform your acts of righteousness in front of others just to be noticed" | Dynamic, natural but "acts of" is added |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering — "Beware of practicing your righteousness" is natural and accurate. PNEUMA's "acts of righteousness" adds a word not in the Greek. EMET's word order is impenetrable.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:2 — "give to the needy" vs. "do alms" vs. "give to the poor" for Greek ἐλεημοσύνην

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "do alms" | Literal rendering of ποιῇς ἐλεημοσύνην |
| LOGOS | "give to the needy" | Dynamic rendering clarifying the act |
| PNEUMA | "give to the poor" | Similar dynamic rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "give to the needy" — ἐλεημοσύνη means charitable giving/almsgiving. "Give to the needy" makes the act clear. "Do alms" (EMET) is archaic; "give to the poor" (PNEUMA) is equally valid but "needy" is slightly broader.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:2 — "sound no trumpet before you" vs. "don't announce it with fanfare"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "do not sound-a-trumpet before you" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "sound no trumpet before you" | Formal, preserving the trumpet image |
| PNEUMA | "don't announce it with fanfare" | Dynamic — "fanfare" captures the sense without the literal trumpet |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "sound no trumpet before you" — whether Jesus refers to an actual practice or uses a vivid metaphor, the trumpet image is striking and memorable. PNEUMA's "fanfare" domesticates the image.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The vivid image is preserved.

---

## 6:7 — "do not heap up empty phrases" vs. "do not babble" vs. "don't babble on and on" for Greek βατταλογήσητε

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "do not babble" | Direct rendering of βατταλογέω (to babble, stammer) |
| LOGOS | "do not heap up empty phrases" | Interpretive — explains the nature of the babbling |
| PNEUMA | "don't babble on and on" | "On and on" adds repetitive emphasis |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "do not heap up empty phrases" — βατταλογέω is a rare word meaning to babble or use vain repetitions. LOGOS's "heap up empty phrases" captures both the repetitive nature and the emptiness of the speech, which the context about "many words" (πολυλογίᾳ) supports. EMET's "babble" is too vague; PNEUMA adds emphasis not in the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The rendering captures the full sense.

---

## 6:7 — "Gentiles" vs. "pagans" for Greek ἐθνικοί

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Gentiles" | Standard rendering |
| LOGOS | "the Gentiles" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "the pagans" | Pejorative rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the Gentiles" — consistent with the resolution at 5:47. ἐθνικοί means non-Jews, and "Gentiles" is the established term.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Consistency maintained.

---

## 6:9 — "Our Father in heaven, hallowed be your name" vs. "Father of us, the one in the heavens, let-be-hallowed the name of you" vs. "Our Father in heaven, may your name be honored as holy"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Father of us, the [one] in the heavens, let-be-hallowed the name of you" | Extremely literal |
| LOGOS | "Our Father in heaven, hallowed be your name" | Traditional liturgical rendering |
| PNEUMA | "Our Father in heaven, may your name be honored as holy" | Dynamic — explains what "hallowed" means |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Our Father in heaven, hallowed be your name" — this is arguably the most widely known prayer in the English-speaking world. The traditional rendering is accurate, recognized, and carries centuries of liturgical resonance. PNEUMA's "may your name be honored as holy" is a valid explanation of ἁγιασθήτω but replaces one of the most iconic phrases in English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The Lord's Prayer is rendered in its traditional form.

---

## 6:10 — "Your kingdom come, your will be done" vs. "May your kingdom come. May your will be carried out"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "let come the kingdom of you; let be done the will of you" | Literal with jussive mood |
| LOGOS | "Your kingdom come, your will be done" | Traditional liturgical form |
| PNEUMA | "May your kingdom come. May your will be carried out" | "May" makes the petition explicit; "carried out" for γενηθήτω |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted the traditional form — "Your kingdom come, your will be done, on earth as it is in heaven." This is universally recognized. PNEUMA's "may" and "carried out" are accurate but break the traditional rhythm.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Traditional Lord's Prayer language preserved.

---

## 6:11 — "Give us this day our daily bread" vs. "The bread of us, the daily, give to us today" vs. "Give us today the bread we need for this day"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The bread of us, the daily, give to us today" | Literal with ἐπιούσιον preserved in position |
| LOGOS | "Give us this day our daily bread" | Traditional rendering |
| PNEUMA | "Give us today the bread we need for this day" | Expanded — "the bread we need" interprets ἐπιούσιον |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Give us this day our daily bread" — the traditional rendering. ἐπιούσιος is a hapax legomenon whose exact meaning is uncertain. "Daily" is the conventional rendering. PNEUMA's "the bread we need for this day" is one valid interpretation but privileges one reading over others.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Traditional Lord's Prayer language preserved.

---

## 6:12 — "debts" vs. "what we owe" for Greek ὀφειλήματα

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the debts of us" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "our debts" | Standard rendering preserving the financial metaphor |
| PNEUMA | "what we owe" | Dynamic — explains the meaning of "debts" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "our debts... our debtors" — Matthew specifically chose the financial metaphor (ὀφειλήματα) where Luke uses "sins" (ἁμαρτίας). This metaphorical choice is theologically significant and should be preserved.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Matthew's specific metaphor preserved.

---

## 6:13 — "deliver us from evil" vs. "deliver us from the evil one" vs. "rescue us from the evil one"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "deliver us from the evil [one]" | τοῦ πονηροῦ as masculine (personal evil, the devil) |
| LOGOS | "deliver us from evil" | τοῦ πονηροῦ as neuter (abstract evil) |
| PNEUMA | "rescue us from the evil one" | Personal reading; "rescue" for ῥῦσαι |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "deliver us from evil" — the Greek τοῦ πονηροῦ is ambiguous between neuter (evil as an abstract force) and masculine (the evil one, i.e., the devil). The traditional English rendering "from evil" preserves this ambiguity. EMET and PNEUMA resolve it in favor of the personal reading, which is defensible but narrows the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Ambiguity preserved in the traditional rendering.

---

## 6:14-15 — "trespasses" vs. "wrongs" for Greek παραπτώματα

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the trespasses of them... the trespasses of you" | Standard with possessive |
| LOGOS | "their trespasses... your trespasses" | Traditional English |
| PNEUMA | "when they wrong you... your wrongs" | Dynamic — verb form and simplified noun |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "their trespasses... your trespasses" — παράπτωμα (literally "a falling aside") is well rendered by "trespass," which has deep roots in English prayer language (cf. the Lord's Prayer in some traditions). PNEUMA's "wrongs" loses the specific theological term.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Traditional term with liturgical resonance.

---

## 6:19-20 — "lay up for yourselves treasures" vs. "treasure up for yourselves treasures" vs. "don't stockpile wealth"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Do not treasure up for yourselves treasures" | Cognate construction preserved (θησαυρίζετε... θησαυρούς) |
| LOGOS | "Do not lay up for yourselves treasures" | Standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "Don't stockpile wealth here on earth" | "Stockpile wealth" — dynamic, modern |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Do not lay up for yourselves treasures" — "lay up" is the established English idiom for θησαυρίζω in this context. PNEUMA's "stockpile wealth" is too commercial. EMET's "treasure up treasures" preserves the Greek cognate but is redundant in English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:19 — "rust" vs. "eating-away" vs. "corrosion" for Greek βρῶσις

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "eating-away" | Literal — βρῶσις means "eating/consumption," not specifically rust |
| LOGOS | "rust" | Traditional rendering, though βρῶσις more broadly means corrosion or consumption |
| PNEUMA | "corrosion" | Broader than "rust" — captures the eating-away sense |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "rust" — while EMET correctly notes that βρῶσις means "eating/consumption" (and could refer to vermin, decay, or corrosion), "rust" is the traditional English rendering paired with "moth" and creates the memorable doublet "moth and rust." The broader sense is noted.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Traditional pairing "moth and rust" preserved.

---

## 6:22-23 — "healthy/bad" vs. "single/evil" vs. "generous/stingy" for Greek ἁπλοῦς/πονηρός

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "single... evil" | Literal Greek meanings preserved with their ambiguity |
| LOGOS | "healthy... bad" | Surface meaning applied to the eye metaphor |
| PNEUMA | "generous... stingy" | Cultural interpretation — Jewish idiom where good eye = generous |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "healthy... bad" — this preserves the surface meaning of the eye metaphor (a healthy eye lets in light; a bad eye does not) while allowing readers to discover the deeper cultural meaning. PNEUMA's "generous/stingy" is likely the correct cultural reading but collapses the metaphor into its interpretation. EMET's "single" is too obscure.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Surface metaphor preserved; cultural depth for annotation.

---

## 6:24 — "money" vs. "mammon" vs. "Money" (capitalized) for Greek μαμωνᾷ

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "mammon" | Transliterated Aramaic loanword, preserving the personification |
| LOGOS | "money" | Translated for clarity |
| PNEUMA | "Money" (capitalized) | Translated but capitalized to preserve the personification |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "money" (lowercase) — the Aramaic μαμωνᾶ is rendered by its meaning for accessibility. The personification is clear from context ("serve... money"). PNEUMA's capitalization is a creative solution but unconventional. EMET's "mammon" preserves the foreign word but is unfamiliar.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Clarity with context-evident personification.

---

## 6:25 — "do not be anxious" vs. "stop worrying" for Greek μὴ μεριμνᾶτε

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "do not be anxious" | Standard rendering of μεριμνάω |
| LOGOS | "do not be anxious" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "stop worrying" | "Stop" implies they are currently worrying; "worrying" is more colloquial |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "do not be anxious" — μεριμνάω means to be anxious or worried. "Do not be anxious" is the standard rendering. PNEUMA's "stop worrying" assumes a current state of worry not explicitly stated in the prohibition.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 6:26 — "birds of the air" vs. "birds of the heaven" vs. "the birds"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the birds of the heaven" | Literal — τὰ πετεινὰ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ |
| LOGOS | "the birds of the air" | Traditional English rendering |
| PNEUMA | "the birds" | Simplified — drops the qualifier |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the birds of the air" — "of the air" renders τοῦ οὐρανοῦ in its atmospheric sense (sky/air) and is the traditional English phrasing. PNEUMA's simplification loses a poetic qualifier.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Traditional poetic phrasing.

---

## 6:27 — "a single hour to the span of his life" vs. "one cubit upon the span of him" vs. "a single hour to your life"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "add upon the span of him one cubit" | Literal — preserves ἡλικίαν (span/stature) and πῆχυν (cubit) |
| LOGOS | "add a single hour to the span of his life" | Interprets ἡλικία as lifespan and πῆχυς metaphorically as a unit of time |
| PNEUMA | "add a single hour to your life" | Same lifespan reading, simplified |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering — ἡλικία can mean either lifespan or stature. The lifespan reading makes more sense in context (adding a cubit to one's height is absurdly large, not small; but adding a small unit to one's life is aptly small). "Hour" renders the metaphorical smallness. EMET's literal "cubit" preserves the measure but obscures the point.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Lifespan interpretation with appropriate unit.

---

## 6:28 — "the lilies of the field" vs. "the wildflowers" for Greek τὰ κρίνα τοῦ ἀγροῦ

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the lilies of the field" | Traditional rendering of κρίνα |
| LOGOS | "the lilies of the field" | Same traditional rendering |
| PNEUMA | "the wildflowers" | Botanical accuracy — κρίνα likely refers to various wildflowers, not cultivated lilies |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the lilies of the field" — while PNEUMA's botanical observation is likely correct, "the lilies of the field" is one of the most beloved phrases in Jesus's teaching. The phrase has become an English literary and spiritual touchstone. Changing it to "wildflowers" sacrifices enormous cultural resonance for botanical precision.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Cultural and literary resonance preserved.

---

## 6:28 — "Consider the lilies" vs. "Observe the lilies" vs. "Look at how the wildflowers grow"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Observe the lilies of the field" | καταμάθετε — "learn thoroughly by observation" |
| LOGOS | "Consider the lilies of the field" | Traditional rendering with contemplative nuance |
| PNEUMA | "Look at how the wildflowers grow" | Dynamic, colloquial |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Consider the lilies of the field" — καταμάθετε means to observe carefully, study, consider. "Consider" captures the contemplative depth better than "look at." This is another phrase with deep English literary resonance.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. "Consider the lilies" is iconic English.

---

## 6:30 — "O you of little faith" vs. "ones of little faith" vs. "How little faith you have!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "[ones] of little faith" | Literal rendering of ὀλιγόπιστοι |
| LOGOS | "O you of little faith" | Formal vocative with traditional phrasing |
| PNEUMA | "How little faith you have!" | Exclamatory, modern |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "O you of little faith" — ὀλιγόπιστοι is a distinctive Matthean compound word. The formal vocative "O you of little faith" preserves its character as a gentle rebuke. PNEUMA's exclamatory version is too sharp.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Distinctive Matthean term rendered formally.

---

## 6:33 — "seek first the kingdom of God and his righteousness" vs. "seek first the kingdom and the righteousness of him" vs. "make the kingdom of God and his righteousness your first pursuit"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "seek first the kingdom and the righteousness of him" | Literal — no "of God" in some readings |
| LOGOS | "seek first the kingdom of God and his righteousness" | Standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "make the kingdom of God and his righteousness your first pursuit" | Expanded — "make... your first pursuit" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering — "seek first the kingdom of God and his righteousness" is clear, accurate, and well-established. PNEUMA's expansion is unnecessary.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:34 — "Sufficient for the day is its own trouble" vs. "Each day has enough trouble as it is"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Sufficient to the day [is] the evil of it" | Literal with "evil" for κακία |
| LOGOS | "Sufficient for the day is its own trouble" | Formal, proverbial English |
| PNEUMA | "Each day has enough trouble as it is" | Modern English proverb-style |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Sufficient for the day is its own trouble" — this rendering has the quality of a proverb, matching the aphoristic nature of the Greek. PNEUMA's version is natural but prosaic. EMET's "evil" is too strong for κακία in this context (where it means trouble/hardship, not moral evil).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Proverbial quality preserved.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Frequency | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA modernizes iconic/liturgical language | 6 occurrences | Rejected — traditional Lord's Prayer and beloved phrases preserved |
| PNEUMA interpretive cultural reading | 2 occurrences | Noted but kept for annotation (e.g., generous/stingy eye) |
| EMET awkward literalism | 5 occurrences | Resolved in favor of natural English |
| LOGOS provides traditional baseline | 14 occurrences | LOGOS most often adopted |
| Lord's Prayer traditional rendering | 5 occurrences | Traditional form preserved throughout |

### Overall Pattern Analysis

Matthew 6 contains the Lord's Prayer (vv.9-13) and the "lilies of the field" discourse (vv.25-34), two of the most culturally embedded passages in English-language Christianity. This context overwhelmingly favored LOGOS's traditional renderings. The Lord's Prayer was preserved in its established English form without exception — this is a text that millions of English speakers know by heart, and departing from the traditional wording requires extraordinary justification that was not present.

PNEUMA's most significant contribution in this chapter was the cultural reading of the "good eye / evil eye" metaphor (vv.22-23), where ἁπλοῦς/πονηρός likely reflects the Jewish idiom of generosity vs. stinginess. This insight is valuable but was judged better suited to annotation than to the translation text itself, which preserves the surface metaphor of a healthy vs. bad eye.

The resolution on "evil" vs. "the evil one" in 6:13 was theologically significant — SOPHIA preserved the traditional ambiguous rendering "from evil" rather than resolving the Greek τοῦ πονηροῦ as personal (the devil) or abstract (evil).

---

*Compiled by SOPHIA, AI Arbiter, 2026-03-05*
